tweets.text:
If you remember back to March, all assets shifted to a negative correlation with oil. 

Oil up(down) = rates up(down) = stocks down(up). 

This brought to life the Semi-stock long + energy barbell trade. 

The vols here suggest that trade may be coming back, just as we approach FOMC next week.

fuentes understands israel’s motivations pretty well by now (took him a while, he was wrong about it for most of the last 3 years)

most of this video is correct. but the last few seconds are not. israel is not doing this to “dominate” the region, and really the situation he’s describing isn’t “domination”

it’s equilibrium.

with iran significantly weakened, saudi/UAE/qatar will dominate the region. not israel. israel is too small, too poor. but with IRGC out of the picture, an equilibrium can and will form where all middle eastern can finally work together constructively using internal resources

it will end the dependence on american funding and middle eastern capital will finally start flowing into the US instead of the other way around

israelis want nothing more than an equilibrium that allows them to be financially independent in the region. that’s it.

tweets.text:
4 minute is a very long time to ask for from someone when they’re in a scrolling mood. you really have to sell it to them very hard in the first few seconds if they don’t already know about it and want to watch it

you don’t HAVE to use sexuality, there are other ways to stop the scroll, but you need SOMETHING. and whatever you use to stop the scroll, all it buys you is a few seconds you can then leverage to “sell” the rest of the video:

1. something visually striking in the first frame that stops the scroll

2. something happens over the next 3 seconds that tells people “you HAVE to keep watching this” (and to make matters worse it has to work with audio muted, especially on X)

3. even if you did (1) and (2) really well, most scrollers don’t want to spend 4 minutes on a video. might have to make it shorter. maybe format it to have multiple parts

4. somewhere in the video it should also have strong bait that gets people to comment/share. like an easter egg that feels out of place that gets people to respond and declare they found it. or a cliffhanger that causes people to beg to find out what happens next. that encourages the algo to show your video to more people, and if (1) and (2) are strong the loop compounds

sorry i know you didn’t ask for tips and you probably know all that already :) just thought some people reading your post might be interested

tweets.text:
I’m starting a new format: Monthly Protocol Reviews.

Let's call them MPR's for short.

These will be a way for me to showcase the best builders in the space.

First up is @SuiNetwork.

Here are all the updates ↓

1. Hashi testnet went live on the 22nd

Hashi lets users borrow against their BTC without moving it off the Bitcoin network.

Collateral is secured by a 2-of-2 multisig, one signature from Hashi's validators and one from a guardian.

25+ partners are building on it ahead of mainnet, and Wave Digital committed to three years of bringing Bitcoin-yield bond products onchain through Hashi.

2. Native SUI staking is live on Coinbase

Rewards accrue directly in the Coinbase account, with no need to choose a validator or manage a separate position.

Sui staking has also been live on Revolut since earlier this year.

This is great for those that cannot deal with the additional hassle from other apps.

3. Sui added confidential transfers (public beta)

Amounts and balances are hidden from public observers, while senders, receivers, and asset issuers keep full visibility.

Exchanges and regulators get risk-tiered access.

No company is moving payroll onchain if competitors can read every payment, so this is a practical solution to that problem.

4. @utila_io added gasless stablecoin transfers

Utila's enterprise users can now send stablecoins on Sui with zero SUI in the wallet.

This is possible due to Sui's gasless stablecoin transfers (added earlier this year).

5. A new record of 6M TPS through Sui Tunnels

In their test run, Sui processed around 2.3B txns in ~20 minutes.

Tunnels handle execution offchain and settle back to Sui, which is how the number gets that high.

It's the largest throughput test the ecosystem has run so far.

6. @0xbeepit added Muse Spark to its Sui trading/prediction agents

Beep says the model outperforms leading models like Opus, GPT, and Gemini on finance tasks.

You can deploy it alongside GPT, Gemini, and other frontier models, and then compare how each performs with your own strategy.

The model is available on both Beep Trading Agent and Predict Agent.

BTC holders, exchange users, and institutions all gained new ways to access and use Sui in July, all within a few weeks.

That's quite an accomplishment for such a short time period.

Hopefully, they continue with this tempo.

Disclosure: I'm a long-time Sui supporter and $SUI holder.

tweets.text:
Didn’t expect Trace to cross 100M receipts so quickly after my last post.

Trace (the public data auditing tool for @datafdn) has now passed 111M receipts in under a month, with 177TB of AI training data audited across 173K contributors.

And all of it comes from apps auditing and registering real-world AI training data.

This activity has already pushed DATA into the top 20 chains for daily fees on @DefiLlama.

Btw, the receipts are anonymized, so the activity can be verified without revealing who contributed the data.

I really like the balance between public proof and private contributors.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Over 100 million registrations in less than 30 days of launch.

That's 100+ million permanent legal receipts for AI training data created on Trace.

So far:
▸ 105M registrations
▸ 171k contributors
▸ 170 TB of data audited
▸ $57k chain fees https://t.co/paAPvaOdS6
tweets.full_text:
Didn’t expect Trace to cross 100M receipts so quickly after my last post.

Trace (the public data auditing tool for @datafdn) has now passed 111M receipts in under a month, with 177TB of AI training data audited across 173K contributors.

And all of it comes from apps auditing and registering real-world AI training data.

This activity has already pushed DATA into the top 20 chains for daily fees on @DefiLlama.

Btw, the receipts are anonymized, so the activity can be verified without revealing who contributed the data.

I really like the balance between public proof and private contributors.

tweets.text:
Contributed to this great piece
Here’s your TL;DR ↓

• Study scope: 71,697 buyers; $91.3B gross acquisitions across ten tokenised dollar-yield products; tokenised RWAs up 179% to $33.8B by July 2026.

• No TradFi flow in the attributable pool: of $12.4B we could attribute to buyer types, 66% was protocol/DAO treasuries and zero dollars were traced to pensions, asset managers, or banks.

• Extreme concentration: wallets writing checks ≥ $1M are ~4% of buyers but account for ~93% of acquired notional.

• Largest institutional tickets go to credit-like products: @centrifuge JAAA median institutional cheque ≈ $29.1M, ~3x the next-largest product.

• USDC is the dominant settlement rail: ~80% ($17.4B) of acquisitions used USDC; USDT accounts for ~$4.4B (mostly via syrupUSDT).

• Buyer base is fresh capital: median first activity dates to mid-2024 (retail ≈ Mar‑2024; institutional ≈ Jul‑2024); few wallets predate 2021.

• Primary subscription dominates: 93–100% of acquisitions are primary mints; genuine DEX entry is 0–6% across products except @ethena sUSDe at 55% DEX-sourced.

• Leverage is limited and concentrated: most products lack lending markets; mF-ONE peaked near 42% borrowed vs notional then unwound to ~8%; AA_FalconXUSDC peaked ~29% and was ~25% outstanding on July 7, 2026.

• Retention skews to credit/treasury: TradFi-like credit funds retain ~68% of buyers at one year; tokenised treasuries ~60%; crypto-native carry/credit products churn to ~28% by year-end.

• Operating hours skew non‑US: EMEA ~42%, APAC ~40%, Americas ~18% by buyer activity window.

• Unattributed institutional capital largely CEX/DeFi-funded after two hops: of $5.17B otherwise-unattributed, ~40% traces to CEX funding (Binance ≈ $1.12B noted), ~38% DeFi-native, ~22% unidentified.

• Net composite APY demanded by large allocators rises with credit-likeness: ≈2.3% on synthetic-dollar carry up to ≈7.4% on gated private-credit funds.

• Retail is headcount, not capital: @Ondo USDY is 95.6% retail by buyer count yet those wallets hold under 1% of USDY value; Ethena sUSDe concentrates most retail presence but retail remains a minority of capital.

• Secondary liquidity is the biggest composability gap: without DEX/secondary depth, RWAs remain subscription products and rarely function as continuous collateral or composable assets.

The onchain demand profile looks like freshly formed crypto-native treasuries, exchanges and funds spinning up purpose-built wallets in 2024 and buying primarily at issuance via USDC rails.

Where lending markets and DEX liquidity exist, holders post collateral and looping creates leverage (seen in mF-ONE and AA_FalconX); where markets don’t exist, capital sits in spot earning base yield.

These dynamics explain concentration, the preference for primary issuance, and why retail shows up in counts but not capital share.

Full read ↓

Contributed to this great piece
Here’s your TL;DR ↓

• Study scope: 71,697 buyers; $91.3B gross acquisitions across ten tokenised dollar-yield products; tokenised RWAs up 179% to $33.8B by July 2026.

• No TradFi flow in the attributable pool: of $12.4B we could attribute to buyer types, 66% was protocol/DAO treasuries and zero dollars were traced to pensions, asset managers, or banks.

• Extreme concentration: wallets writing checks ≥ $1M are ~4% of buyers but account for ~93% of acquired notional.

• Largest institutional tickets go to credit-like products: @centrifuge JAAA median institutional cheque ≈ $29.1M, ~3x the next-largest product.

• USDC is the dominant settlement rail: ~80% ($17.4B) of acquisitions used USDC; USDT accounts for ~$4.4B (mostly via syrupUSDT).

• Buyer base is fresh capital: median first activity dates to mid-2024 (retail ≈ Mar‑2024; institutional ≈ Jul‑2024); few wallets predate 2021.

• Primary subscription dominates: 93–100% of acquisitions are primary mints; genuine DEX entry is 0–6% across products except @ethena sUSDe at 55% DEX-sourced.

• Leverage is limited and concentrated: most products lack lending markets; mF-ONE peaked near 42% borrowed vs notional then unwound to ~8%; AA_FalconXUSDC peaked ~29% and was ~25% outstanding on July 7, 2026.

• Retention skews to credit/treasury: TradFi-like credit funds retain ~68% of buyers at one year; tokenised treasuries ~60%; crypto-native carry/credit products churn to ~28% by year-end.

• Operating hours skew non‑US: EMEA ~42%, APAC ~40%, Americas ~18% by buyer activity window.

• Unattributed institutional capital largely CEX/DeFi-funded after two hops: of $5.17B otherwise-unattributed, ~40% traces to CEX funding (Binance ≈ $1.12B noted), ~38% DeFi-native, ~22% unidentified.

• Net composite APY demanded by large allocators rises with credit-likeness: ≈2.3% on synthetic-dollar carry up to ≈7.4% on gated private-credit funds.

• Retail is headcount, not capital: @Ondo USDY is 95.6% retail by buyer count yet those wallets hold under 1% of USDY value; Ethena sUSDe concentrates most retail presence but retail remains a minority of capital.

tweets.text:
#uranium #nuclear $SYH.V $SYHBF

Skyharbour has added another 149,439 hectares through low-cost staking, expanding its Athabasca Basin portfolio to more than 682,000 hectares across 44 uranium properties—one of Canada’s largest.

This is much more than acreage for acreage’s sake. It strengthens Skyharbour’s two-engine strategy:

⛏️ Aggressive exploration at the high-grade Moore Project and Russell Lake JV with Denison Mines

🤝 A growing prospect-generator portfolio that can attract partners, preserve royalties and equity upside, and limit shareholder-funded exploration costs

Several of the newly acquired projects already contain compelling targets:

▪️ Carter North covers interpreted extensions of the same conductive corridors hosting Arrow and Triple R
▪️ Ford Lake sits near Key Lake, Millennium, Phoenix and Gryphon
▪️ Pine hosts historical uranium mineralization and numerous largely untested conductors
▪️ Cree North is positioned between Denison’s Phoenix deposit and Cameco’s McArthur River project

Skyharbour’s existing earn-in agreements already provide for potentially more than $76M in partner-funded exploration and over $45M in cash and share payments if fully completed.

More land, more targets, and more potential partners while drilling continues at the flagship projects.

That looks like excellent positioning for the next stage of the uranium bull market.

Disclosure: Paid partnership with Skyharbour Resources. Not financial advice. Do your own research.

#uranium #nuclear $SYH.V $SYHBF

Skyharbour has added another 149,439 hectares through low-cost staking, expanding its Athabasca Basin portfolio to more than 682,000 hectares across 44 uranium properties—one of Canada’s largest.

This is much more than acreage for acreage’s sake. It strengthens Skyharbour’s two-engine strategy:

⛏️ Aggressive exploration at the high-grade Moore Project and Russell Lake JV with Denison Mines

🤝 A growing prospect-generator portfolio that can attract partners, preserve royalties and equity upside, and limit shareholder-funded exploration costs

Several of the newly acquired projects already contain compelling targets:

▪️ Carter North covers interpreted extensions of the same conductive corridors hosting Arrow and Triple R
▪️ Ford Lake sits near Key Lake, Millennium, Phoenix and Gryphon
▪️ Pine hosts historical uranium mineralization and numerous largely untested conductors
▪️ Cree North is positioned between Denison’s Phoenix deposit and Cameco’s McArthur River project

Skyharbour’s existing earn-in agreements already provide for potentially more than $76M in partner-funded exploration and over $45M in cash and share payments if fully completed.

More land, more targets, and more potential partners while drilling continues at the flagship projects.

That looks like excellent positioning for the next stage of the uranium bull market.

Disclosure: Paid partnership with Skyharbour Resources. Not financial advice. Do your own research.

tweets.text:
Everyone agrees stablecoins are the most obvious use case crypto has ever produced.

Yet I hardly see anyone going for the second and third order effect markets.

If USD stablecoins are that obvious, non-USD stablecoins should be just as obvious. And if that's true, so is the entire FX market sitting behind them.

FX is the single largest market on earth. Roughly $10 trillion in daily volume. And while onchain EUR/USD trades are starting to pick up, they barely exist yet.

Spot DEX volumes hardly ever exceed $30M a day. That's a 333,000x difference. This market essentially doesn't even exist in crypto yet.

And the case for moving it onchain is the same old story:

Instant settlement instead of waiting days, permissionless markets that never close, the same rates for a small business as for a bulge bracket bank and horrendous fees going down to fractions of a cent.

It surely will take time just as USD stablecoins did, but rarely is something this obvious also this big.

tweets.text:
Technically, the deal has not officially collapsed yet. But in reality, it is over. The Prime Minister and the Minister of Defense have already made it clear that the transaction will not be approved for national security reasons.

The fact that a company was willing to pay around $35 per share, with the possibility that other bidders could have offered even more, says a lot about $ZIM ’s value.

When an acquirer is willing to pay that kind of price, it’s usually because they believe the business is worth significantly more than what they are paying, often 50% or more above the purchase price. It gives you an idea of the underlying value they saw in ZIM.

tweets.text:
Most of the market is priced for a world that's coming to an end.

Traditional software, legacy platforms, much of the SaaS layer of the S&P 500… all built on the assumption that humans will remain the primary customers.

AI agents don't buy software the way we do. They don't need dashboards, subscriptions, or logins. And once the buyer changes, so does everything built around them.

The investable opportunity ahead is the agentic economy… what gets built for agents as customers.

That's where the next wave of capital ends up, and most people aren't positioned for it.

tweets.text: In typical organizations, most decisions are made either autocratically, by a top-down leader, or democratically, where everyone shares their opinions and those opinions that have the most support are implemented. Both systems produce inferior decision making. That’s because the best decisions are made by an idea meritocracy with believability-weighted decision making, in which the most capable people work through their disagreements with other capable people who have thought independently about what is true and what to do about it. #principleoftheday

tweets.text:
People say AI is expensive.

Let me break it down. I pay $200 a month for Claude and get PhD-level intelligence on every subject known to humanity.

Now price a human. Globally, on average, maybe $10,000 a year in food to run one brain. Plus the twenty odd years it takes to grow it first.

So it's $10,000 a year for human-level compute, versus $200 a month for intelligence that clears any human on any topic.

That's why it's scaling this fast. The price of human intelligence is just uneconomical compared to machines.

Generally a single digit % recovery is considered a “positive outcome” for large DPRK exploit.

A recovery bounty does not incentivize anyone who is actually competent since after freezing funds recovery takes many months or years via LE or civil cases thus all of the time spent initially up front for a lower chance of recovery is not attractive enough by itself.

thread_tweets.text:
Both yes and no.

It's unrealistic to expect one person to manage a large DPRK exploit alone (paid or unpaid).

With a grant, sure someone could invest a few extra hours and potentially get funds frozen.

But there are so many external variables: law enforcement, timing, jurisdiction.

You need a full team, and even then costs are outrageous (hours add up fast).

Real progress could only happen through major collaboration across ecosystems and changes in international legislation to smooth out cross-border complexity.

tweets.text:
I’m increasingly convinced that:

Over the next five years, chains that prioritise security-first neutrality and focus on building objective/publicly verifiable defense against hacks will outperform those that don’t.

Chains can do this and still remain a Stage 1 rollup is very useful.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
How to and how not to censor an attacker on a Stage 1 rollup (based on our experience in Security Councils) 🧵

tl;dr: our recommendation is for emergency actions to be initiated by the operator itself. This won't affect the Stage as long as the pause is automatically expiring
tweets.full_text:
